---
title: "DATA3888 Group Report"
author:
        - name: "Finance Group 21"
          affiliation: "The University of Sydney"

lang: en

format:
  html:
    theme:
      light: lux
      dark: darkly
    css:
      - https://use.fontawesome.com/releases/v5.0.6/css/all.css
      - paper.css
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    number-depth: 3
    code-tools: true
    code-fold: true
    link-citations: true
    embed-resources: true
bibliography: references.bib
reference-section-title: "References"
execute:
  echo: true
  tidy: true
---

## Executive Summary

This report investigates whether a heterogeneous ensemble of forecasting models can systematically outperform classical benchmarks for 2-minute-ahead realised volatility prediction. To do so, we evaluate four models: The GARCH family as a classical benchmark, HAR-X as an augmented industry baseline, and two ensemble approaches: a Residual Stacker with QLike-optimised correction and a Graph Neural Network (GNN) with a learned HAR blend. These models are assessed on RMSPE and QLike loss metrics, which together capture both point accuracy and the asymmetric cost of forecast errors relevant to options pricing.

The Residual Stacker achieves the lowest QLike score (0.045), outperforming all benchmarks including HAR-X (0.051) and GARCH (0.056), while the GNN Hybrid achieves the lowest RMSPE (0.412), a \~24% reduction over HAR-X(0.542), but at the cost of elevated QLike (0.072). These results suggest that model selection in volatility forecasting cannot be reduced to a single metric and the appropriate ensemble depends on whether the downstream task penalises miscalibration or magnitude error. The analysis is accompanied by an interactive Plotly Dash monitoring tool to enable inspection of model predictions across stocks and evaluate per-stock risk decomposition.

## Background

### History

Volatility forecasting sits at the core of options pricing and risk management. The Black-Scholes model, the foundation of modern derivatives pricing, requires a forward-looking volatility estimate that historical averages alone cannot reliably provide. GARCH-family models addressed this by inferring conditional volatility from daily return sequences, establishing the early standard for volatility forecasting [@ghysels2006predicting]. The availability of high-frequency limit order book data, however, made more granular approaches possible.

[@corsi2009har] formalised this with the Heterogeneous Autoregressive model of Realised Volatility (HAR-RV), which regresses future realised volatility on lagged RV at multiple horizons, capturing the heterogeneous time scales at which different market participants operate. Despite its parsimony, HAR-RV has proven consistently difficult to outperform out-of-sample, cementing its status as the de facto industry benchmark. For Optiver, a global electronic market maker pricing and hedging options in real time, it represents the practical baseline against which any proposed improvement must be evaluated.

### Motivation & Research Question

HAR-RV's limitations are well-documented, yet its out-of-sample robustness has proven remarkably difficult to overcome. Rather than replacing HAR, this study investigates whether heterogeneous ensemble methods can exploit HAR's robustness as a foundation. The ensemble models extend upon it with nonlinear correction and cross-stock structure to capture signals that a linear, stock-independent model cannot. Both ensemble approaches proposed here are constrained to improve on HAR rather than circumvent it, as such any measured gain is more plausibly attributable to what the ensemble adds beyond the baseline. This motivates the central research question:

*Can a heterogeneous ensemble of volatility forecasting models systematically outperform individual classical benchmarks for short-term volatility forecasting using high-frequency order book data?*

### Exploratory Data Analysis

The Optiver dataset covers 112 stocks at per-second resolution, with each 10-minute window split into an 8-minute input period and a 2-minute forecast target. The 2-minute target balances statistical reliability, predictive feasibility, and tradability. A shorter target (e.g. 30 seconds) would be dominated by microstructure noise and leave insufficient time for model inference and order execution. A longer target (e.g. 5 minutes) produces a smoother volatility estimate but reduces the actionability of the forecast. At 120 seconds the window contains enough price observations for a meaningful RV estimate while still leaving time for a trader to act.

The 112 stocks are highly heterogeneous: mean bid-ask spreads range across three orders of magnitude and mean per-second volume spans from double digits to tens of thousands. A spread of 0.001 carries very different economic meaning depending on the stock's price level, making per-stock model fitting and within-stock z-scoring essential before any pooled modelling.

The forecast target, log RV over seconds 480–599, has a pooled mean of −6.612 and standard deviation of 0.709 across 343,145 windows. The log transformation yields an approximately Gaussian distribution, supporting OLS fitting in log-space. Roughly 0.1% of windows fall below a noise floor at $log(10⁻⁴) = −9.21$, too few to distort training. Note that `time_id` values are anonymous window identifiers rather than a sequential time index, so lagged relationships across windows cannot be constructed from `time_id` alone.

Volatility co-moves strongly across stocks, but contemporaneously rather than predictively. Pairwise log RV correlations average 0.672, with 97% of pairs above 0.5; however, Granger causality and lead-lag tests find no cross-stock predictive structure at any lag (details in Appendix X). A graph neural network can therefore exploit same-time co-movement to share information across stocks within a window, but lagged cross-stock features would add noise rather than signal.

## Methodology

```{python}
#| label: fig-workflow
#| fig-cap: "Workflow Diagram of our study"
#| echo: True

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.image as mpimg

img = mpimg.imread("workflow diagram.png")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.imshow(img)
ax.axis("off")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Preprocessing

From the raw order book, six features are computed per second: WAP, log return, bid-ask spread, total volume, price spread, and depth imbalance. Since some seconds contain no order book updates, the data is forward-filled to maintain a continuous one-second grid before feature computation. The forecast target is defined as:

$$
\text{RV} = \sqrt{\sum r_t^2}
$$

over seconds 480–599, with the model trained to predict $logRV$, floored at $10⁻⁴$. Five-fold cross-validation is constructed using GroupShuffleSplit on `time_id` (80/20 split) since `time_id` is non-sequential and arbitrary. As such, grouping by `stock_id` ensures the split tests generalisation rather than temporal extrapolation.

### Evaluation Strategy

All models are evaluated under 5-fold cross-validation with GroupKFold on stock_id. Out-of-fold predictions are concatenated across all five folds to produce a single set of predictions covering the entire dataset, on which RMSPE and QLike are computed. Pairwise Diebold–Mariano tests with Newey-West HAC variance estimation are conducted on these out-of-fold residuals across all model combinations under both metrics. The Newey-West HAC variance estimator with lag truncation $h = n^{1/3}$ is used to account for serial correlation in forecast errors, which is expected given the persistence of realised volatility across consecutive trading windows. Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction is applied to the resulting 12 simultaneous tests, preferred over Bonferroni given the shared data and fold structure, keeping only the significant results after correction.

```{python}
#| label: fig-rmpse-vs-qlike
#| fig-cap: "RMSPE and QLike loss functions plotted against the forecast-to-realised volatility ratio."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

r = np.linspace(0.05, 4.0, 500)
rmspe = np.abs(1 - r)
qlike = 1/r + np.log(r) - 1

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5), dpi=100)
fig.patch.set_facecolor('white')

ax.plot(
    r, rmspe,
    color='#3266ad',
    linewidth=2.5,
    label='RMSPE  |1 − r|'
)

ax.plot(
    r, qlike,
    color='#d85a30',
    linewidth=2.5,
    linestyle='--',
    label='QLIKE  1/r + ln(r) − 1'
)


ax.axvline(
    x=1,
    color='grey',
    linewidth=0.8,
    linestyle=':'
)

ax.set_xlim(0, 4)
ax.set_ylim(0, 5)
ax.set_xlabel(r'r = $F_t$ / $RV_t$', fontsize=13)
ax.set_ylabel('Per-observation loss', fontsize=13)
ax.set_title(
    'RMSPE vs QLIKE Loss Functions for Realised Volatility',
    fontsize=14,
    pad=20
)

fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.1, right=0.98, top=0.90, bottom=0.18)

ax.legend(frameon=False, fontsize=10, loc='upper right')

ax.text(
    0.25, 0.7,
    '← Underprediction',
    transform=ax.transAxes,
    fontsize=11,
    color='grey',
    ha='center'
)

ax.text(
    0.75, 0.7,
    'Overprediction →',
    transform=ax.transAxes,
    fontsize=11,
    color='grey',
    ha='center'
)

ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax.grid(alpha=0.15)
plt.show()
```

Two metrics are used in combination, as neither alone is sufficient. As seen in @fig-rmpse-vs-qlike, RMSPE, defined as $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{y_i-\hat{y}_i}{y_i}\right)^2}$ , measures proportional prediction error symmetrically. QLike, defined as $\frac{\sigma}{\hat{\sigma}} - \log\left(\frac{\sigma}{\hat{\sigma}}\right) - 1$ , penalises under-prediction more heavily, reflecting the asymmetric cost of underestimating risk in options pricing. [@patton2011volatility]. Although a low QLike can be achieved by a model that systematically overpredicts, due to the fact the loss function is lenient toward overestimation; it does not, on its own, indicate forecast accuracy. RMSPE is therefore needed to confirm that predictions are close to realised values, while QLike ensures that residual errors lean toward the conservative side. The preferred model is one that achieves both low RMSPE and low QLike.

### Benchmark Models

#### GARCH

The Generalised Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity (GARCH) family acts as a classical benchmark for forecasting volatility. These GARCH models work by estimating the conditional variance of returns given the historical market information [@engle_garch101; @vlab_garch]. These models are still used widely in industry due to their ability to deal with volatility clusters and heteroskedasticity within financial return data [@engle_garch101]. Despite the emergence of machine learning and other realised volatility approaches, GARCH remains a standard benchmark for which other more complex models are evaluated against.

The GARCH model used in this study is a GARCH(1,1) specification, fitted independently per (`stock_id`, `time_id`), using logarithmic returns derived from Weighted Average Price (WAP). The model works by taking the first 480 seconds of returns as inputs and fits a GARCH(1,1) specification onto it, following the formula:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha \sigma_{t-1}^2 + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2 $$

where $\varepsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t$ and $z_t$ is a standard noise process. [@vlab_garch]

Once fitted, the model produces a multi-step forecast over the next 120 seconds by projecting the conditional variance forward recursively, which is then aggregated to produce a single realised-volatility estimate.

#### HAR-X

HAR-X serves as the augmented industry baseline, extending the HAR-RV framework of [@corsi2009har] by incorporating order-book microstructure features alongside realised volatility lags. The model regresses log-realised volatility over the forecast window on three heterogeneous RV components: the full 8-minute input window, the final 2-minute bucket, and the intermediate 4-minute window which translates volatility persistence across intraday horizons following Andersen et al. (2003). [@andersen2003realized]

A meaningful ensemble evaluation requires a baseline that already exploits the most accessible predictive signal. By augmenting HAR with bid-ask spread, total order-book volume, and volume imbalance, HAR-X absorbs the straightforward gains from microstructure information that a pure RV model cannot access. Any further improvement achieved by the ensemble approaches therefore reflects nonlinear or cross-stock structure that a linear, stock-independent model cannot capture making HAR-X a more rigorous point of comparison than HAR alone.

The model is estimated via OLS in log-volatility space with predictions exponentiated back to the RV scale, consistent with the log-normal treatment adopted throughout.

### Ensemble Models

#### Residual Stacker with QLike-optimised correction

The Residual Stacker is a two-stage ensemble that augments the HAR-RV baseline with a LightGBM residual correction layer. It was selected in this study for its demonstrated speed advantage over XGBoost and GBDT alternatives at equivalent predictive accuracy [@ke2017lightgbm]. In the first stage, per-stock HAR predictions are generated via nested 5-fold cross validation to produce out-of-fold residuals on a training set. This acts as a stacking design that prevents data leakage in the correction stage. In the second stage, LightGBM is trained to predict these residuals using 42 engineered features derived from the order book, selected via a three-stage pipeline. This consists of mutual information filtering, SHAP-based importance ranking, and permutation importance, spanning realised volatility lags, bid-ask spread dynamics, return distribution moments, and cross-stock z-scores [@wang2024feature]. The final prediction combines both stages:

$$
\hat{y} = \exp(\log \sigma_{\text{HAR}} + f_{\text{LGB}}(x))
$$

The correction model is then trained directly on a custom QLike objective with analytic gradient and Hessian. This architecture is designed so that any improvement over HAR primarily reflects the nonlinear structure in the residuals that OLS cannot capture, isolating the contribution of machine learning to volatility forecasting.

#### Spatio-Temporal GNN with learned HAR blend

The spatio-temporal GNN addresses HAR's core limitation: it treats every stock independently. In reality, volatility shocks co-move across stocks, and spatio-temporal GNNs have demonstrated superior performance over traditional models in cross-sectional time series forecasting by explicitly modelling both spatial dependencies between nodes and temporal dynamics within each node [@alshahil2023stgnn].

The model encodes each stock's 8-bucket sequence using a Mamba selective state-space temporal encoder, then constructs a dynamic learned graph where each stock attends to its top ten most relevant neighbours via GATv2 message passing. A cross-stock attention layer captures any residual market-wide co-movement that the sparse graph misses.

A linear HAR branch runs in parallel, and a sigmoid-gated scalar weight w blends the two paths:

$$
\hat{y} = w \cdot \hat{y}_{\text{HAR}} + (1 - w) \cdot \hat{y}_{\text{GNN}}
$$

If the GNN contributes nothing, w converges to 1 and the model reduces to HAR. This makes the architecture self-regularising and directly answers the research question: any improvement over the benchmark is attributable to the cross-stock structure the GNN adds. Hence, the GNN does not replace the HAR model, it is constrained to improve upon it.

## Results

```{python}
#| label: fig-model-comparison
#| fig-cap: "Out-of-sample forecast evaluation across models. QLIKE and RMSPE computed on realised variance. Brackets denote Diebold-Mariano significance vs HAR-X (*** p < 0.01)."

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mtick
from pathlib import Path

BASE_DIR = Path.cwd().parent
EVAL_DIR = BASE_DIR / "outputs" / "evaluation"

dm_df = pd.read_csv(EVAL_DIR / "dm_tests_all_models_corrected.csv")
base = pd.read_csv(EVAL_DIR / "base_predictions.csv")

model_names = ["HAR-X", "QLIKE-LGB", "GNN", "GARCH"]
colors      = ["steelblue", "seagreen", "tomato", "sienna"]
EPS         = 1e-10

required_cols = ["actual_rv"] + [f"pred_{m}" for m in model_names]
missing = [c for c in required_cols if c not in base.columns]
if missing:
    raise ValueError(f"Missing columns in base: {missing}")

actual = base["actual_rv"].values

def rmspe(y, p):
    y = np.maximum(y, EPS)
    p = np.maximum(p, EPS)
    return np.sqrt(np.mean(((y - p) / y) ** 2))

def qlike(y, p):
    y = np.maximum(y, EPS)
    p = np.maximum(p, EPS)
    return np.mean(y / p - np.log(y / p) - 1)

rmspe_vals = []
qlike_vals = []
for m in model_names:
    pred = base[f"pred_{m}"].values
    rmspe_vals.append(rmspe(actual, pred))
    qlike_vals.append(qlike(actual, pred))

def get_star(metric, m1, m2):
    row = dm_df[
        (dm_df["metric"] == metric) &
        (((dm_df["model_1"] == m1) & (dm_df["model_2"] == m2)) |
         ((dm_df["model_1"] == m2) & (dm_df["model_2"] == m1)))
    ]
    if row.empty:
        return ""
    return row["sig"].iloc[0]

def bracket_base(values, pad=1.10):
    return max(values) * pad

def add_bracket(ax, x1, x2, y, text, arm_frac=0.015):
    arm = y * arm_frac
    ax.plot(
        [x1, x1, x2, x2],
        [y, y + arm, y + arm, y],
        color="black", lw=1, clip_on=False,
    )
    ax.text(
        (x1 + x2) / 2, y + arm * 1.5,
        text, ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=13,
    )

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8, 5))
x         = np.arange(len(model_names))
harx_idx  = model_names.index("HAR-X")

axes[0].bar(x, qlike_vals, color=colors, alpha=0.8)
axes[0].set_title("QLIKE")
for i, v in enumerate(qlike_vals):
    axes[0].text(i, v, f"{v:.4f}", ha="center", va="bottom")

best_q = int(np.argmin(qlike_vals))
sig_q  = get_star("QLIKE", "HAR-X", model_names[best_q])
if sig_q:
    add_bracket(axes[0], harx_idx, best_q, bracket_base(qlike_vals), sig_q)

axes[0].set_xticks(x)
axes[0].set_xticklabels(model_names)
axes[0].tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=8)
axes[0].yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FormatStrFormatter("%.3f"))
axes[0].set_ylim(0, max(qlike_vals) * 1.22)

axes[1].bar(x, rmspe_vals, color=colors, alpha=0.8)
axes[1].set_title("RMSPE")
for i, v in enumerate(rmspe_vals):
    axes[1].text(i, v, f"{v:.4f}", ha="center", va="bottom")

best_r = int(np.argmin(rmspe_vals))
sig_r  = get_star("RMSPE", "HAR-X", model_names[best_r])
if sig_r:
    add_bracket(axes[1], harx_idx, best_r, bracket_base(rmspe_vals), sig_r)

axes[1].set_xticks(x)
axes[1].set_xticklabels(model_names)
axes[1].tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=8)
axes[1].yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FormatStrFormatter("%.3f"))
axes[1].set_ylim(0, max(rmspe_vals) * 1.22)

plt.tight_layout()
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.12, wspace=0.35)
plt.show()
```

The results, as seen above in @fig-model-comparison show that model performance is not uniformly improved by greater complexity. HAR-X remains an interpretable and balanced reference point with the addition of exogenous liquidity and order-book variables, achieving an RMSPE of 0.5416 and QLike of 0.0511. This suggests that short-horizon volatility is shaped not only by volatility persistence, but also by immediate trading frictions such as spread, volume, and order imbalance.

```{python}
#| label: fig-dm-table
#| fig-cap: "Diebold-Mariano pairwise tests with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction. Positive DM stat indicates Model 2 is better; significance stars reflect BH-corrected p-values."
#| echo: True

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
from matplotlib.lines import Line2D
from pathlib import Path
from statsmodels.stats.multitest import multipletests

BASE_DIR = Path.cwd().parent
EVAL_DIR = BASE_DIR / "outputs" / "evaluation"

# Load raw DM results and apply BH correction inline
dm_df = pd.read_csv(EVAL_DIR / "dm_tests_all_models.csv")

for metric in ["RMSPE", "QLIKE"]:
    mask = dm_df["metric"] == metric
    _, pvals_corrected, _, _ = multipletests(
        dm_df.loc[mask, "p_value"].values, method="fdr_bh"
    )
    dm_df.loc[mask, "p_value_corrected"] = pvals_corrected

def sig_stars(p):
    if p < 0.001: return "***"
    if p < 0.01:  return "**"
    if p < 0.05:  return "*"
    return ""

# ── Build display dataframe ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
rows = []
for _, row in dm_df.iterrows():
    rows.append({
        "Metric":       row["metric"],
        "Model 1":      row["model_1"],
        "Model 2":      row["model_2"],
        "DM Stat":      f"{row['dm_stat']:+.4f}",
        "p-value":      f"{row['p_value']:.4f}",
        "p-corrected":  f"{row['p_value_corrected']:.4f}",
        "Sig":          sig_stars(row["p_value_corrected"]),
        "Better Model": row["model_2"] if row["dm_stat"] > 0 else row["model_1"],
    })
display_df = pd.DataFrame(rows)

# ── Colour mapping ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
model_colors = {
    "HAR-X":     "steelblue",
    "QLIKE-LGB": "seagreen",
    "GNN":       "tomato",
    "GARCH":     "sienna",
}
sig_colors = {
    "***": "#2ecc71",
    "**":  "#f39c12",
    "*":   "#e67e22",
    "":    "#aaaaaa",
}

# ── Layout ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
col_labels = ["Metric", "Model 1", "Model 2", "DM Stat",
              "p-value", "p-corrected", "Sig", "Better Model"]
col_widths = [0.08, 0.11, 0.11, 0.10, 0.10, 0.13, 0.06, 0.13]
n_rows     = len(display_df)

fig_height = 0.42 * (n_rows + 1.5)
fig, ax    = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, fig_height))
ax.axis("off")

fig.patch.set_facecolor("white")
ax.set_facecolor("white")

# ── Header ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
x_cursor = 0.0
for label, w in zip(col_labels, col_widths):
    ax.text(x_cursor + w / 2, 1.0, label,
            ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9,
            fontweight="bold", color="white",
            transform=ax.transAxes,
            bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.3",
                      facecolor="#2c3e50", edgecolor="none"))
    x_cursor += w

# ── Rows ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
row_height = 1.0 / (n_rows + 1)
for i, (_, row) in enumerate(display_df.iterrows()):
    y  = 1.0 - (i + 1) * row_height - row_height * 0.1
    bg = "#f7f9fc" if i % 2 == 0 else "#eaf0f6"

    ax.add_patch(mpatches.FancyBboxPatch(
        (0, y - row_height * 0.45), 1, row_height * 0.9,
        boxstyle="square,pad=0",
        facecolor=bg, edgecolor="none",
        transform=ax.transAxes, zorder=0,
    ))

    x_cursor = 0.0
    for col, w in zip(col_labels, col_widths):
        val   = row[col]
        color = "black"
        fw    = "normal"

        if col == "Metric":
            color = "#2c3e50"
            fw    = "bold"
        elif col in ("Model 1", "Model 2"):
            color = model_colors.get(val, "black")
            fw    = "bold"
        elif col == "Sig":
            color = sig_colors.get(val, "#aaaaaa")
            fw    = "bold"
        elif col == "Better Model":
            color = model_colors.get(val, "black")
            fw    = "bold"
        elif col == "DM Stat":
            color = "#27ae60" if row["DM Stat"].startswith("+") else "#c0392b"

        ax.text(x_cursor + w / 2, y, val,
                ha="center", va="center",
                fontsize=8.5, color=color, fontweight=fw,
                transform=ax.transAxes)
        x_cursor += w

# ── Divider between RMSPE and QLIKE blocks ────────────────────────────────────
divider_y = 1.0 - 6 * row_height - row_height * 0.55
ax.add_artist(Line2D(
    [0, 1], [divider_y, divider_y],
    transform=ax.transAxes,
    color="#2c3e50", linewidth=1.5, zorder=5,
))

# ── Legend ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
legend_elements = [
    mpatches.Patch(facecolor=sig_colors["***"], label="*** p < 0.001"),
    mpatches.Patch(facecolor=sig_colors["**"],  label="**  p < 0.01"),
    mpatches.Patch(facecolor=sig_colors["*"],   label="*   p < 0.05"),
    mpatches.Patch(facecolor=sig_colors[""],    label="Not significant"),
]
ax.legend(handles=legend_elements, loc="lower center",
          bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, -0.08), ncol=4,
          fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.0,
          facecolor="white", edgecolor="none",
          title="Significance (BH-corrected)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

```

From @fig-model-comparison, the residual stacker, labelled as QLIKE-LGB, achieves the lowest QLike at 0.0450 in the model comparison. @fig-dm-table strengthens this result by showing that its QLike advantage is statistically significant after Benjamini-Hochberg correction against HAR-X, GNN, and GARCH. This means the improvement is not only numerically better, but also statistically supported in the pairwise forecast comparison. Since QLIKE-LGB is trained using a QLike-optimised residual correction, it is better aligned with avoiding poorly calibrated volatility forecasts, making it more suitable for risk-sensitive options pricing and risk management. Its RMSPE of 0.5501 also remains close to HAR-X’s 0.5416, suggesting only a limited sacrifice in point accuracy.

By contrast, the GNN achieves the lowest RMSPE of 0.4121. The Diebold-Mariano results in @fig-dm-table also favour GNN under RMSPE against HAR-X, QLIKE-LGB, and GARCH, confirming that its point-forecast advantage is statistically meaningful. This supports the predictive value of modelling cross-stock volatility spillovers. However, the GNN records the weakest QLike of 0.0725, and the QLike-based Diebold-Mariano comparisons favour HAR-X, QLIKE-LGB, and even GARCH over GNN. This shows that closer average point forecasts can still be poorly calibrated under a risk-sensitive loss. Therefore, no model dominates: GNN is best for point accuracy, QLIKE-LGB for conservative calibration, and HAR-X remains the most interpretable balanced benchmark despite slightly weaker headline metrics.

#### Product

```{python}
#| label: fig-dash-app
#| fig-cap: "DashApp landing page showing model predictions across stocks."
#| echo: True

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.image as mpimg

img = mpimg.imread("../DashApp/dashapp.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.imshow(img)
ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

```

While the research question focuses on whether heterogeneous ensembles outperform classical volatility forecasting benchmarks, practitioners also need to understand when those improvements occur and whether they are robust across different stocks and market conditions. To make the results visualisable to the user, an interactive volatility monitoring dashboard was built using Plotly Dash, as illustrated in @fig-dash-app, with more windows shown in the appendix.

The Dash App helps explore the research question by allowing the user to compare the benchmark models (GARCH and HAR-X) to the ensemble models (GNN and Residual Stacker). By visualising the actual realised volatility alongside the model predictions, the dashboard highlights situations where ensemble methods provide superior forecasts compared to the benchmarks, revealing patterns to users that aggregate metrics alone cannot capture. This enables practitioners to interpret the models’ relative strengths and apply the most appropriate forecasting approach depending on the trading environment and risk priorities.

## Discussion

### Limitation

Several limitations are worth noting. First, the time IDs themselves are non-sequential, so the ordering of windows cannot be treated as a clean time series. This limits the ability to model genuine intraday trends or carry information across windows in a principled way. Beyond that, the analysis covers a single dataset of 112 stocks at one forecast horizon, so it is unclear how well the findings carry over to other market conditions, or longer horizons. The anonymity of the stocks also prevents any sector or market-based analysis, meaning the model's structure cannot be validated against known economic relationships between real-world market sectors. Ensemble models are harder to interpret and difficult to explain, which is a practical concern in fast-moving trading environments. 	

### Future Work

Several directions emerge naturally from this study's findings and limitations. The metric-dependent trade-off between the Residual Stacker and GNN suggests that a unified model jointly optimising RMSPE and QLike rather than treating them as separate objectives could yield more balanced forecasts. The current analysis is confined to a single dataset at a fixed 2-minute horizon across anonymised stocks. Evaluating performance across multiple forecast horizons and real-world named securities would establish whether the ensemble advantage is horizon-specific and whether it holds across identifiable sector groupings. The non-sequential nature of `time_id` also prevents genuine temporal modelling across windows. Future work could explore whether a dataset with true temporal ordering enables richer sequential architectures.

## Conclusion

This study set out to investigate whether a heterogeneous ensemble of forecasting models can systematically outperform classical benchmarks for 2-minute-ahead realised volatility prediction. The evidence supports an affirmative but nuanced answer. The Residual Stacker achieves an 11.9% reduction in QLike over HAR-X, and the GNN Hybrid achieves a 23.9% reduction in RMSPE, both statistically significant under Diebold-Mariano testing. High-frequency order book data contains predictive structure beyond what classical linear models capture, and ensemble methods are effective at extracting it. However, no single ensemble dominates across both metrics, suggesting that choosing appropriate forecasting architecture is to be resolved with evaluating the downstream task.

## Student Contributions

| Student | ID | Contributions |
|---|---|---|
| Rahib Taha | 530559445 | - Developed the residual stacker model <br> - Conducted final Diebold-Mariano testing and produced result section plots <br> - Post-processed artifacts and CSV outputs <br> - Hosted and managed the GitHub shared codebase <br> - Wrote presentation script and handled Q&A <br> - Proposed the research question <br> - Wrote and edited the report |
| Thanh Hieu Do | 540914928 | - Preprocessed data and explored modelling approaches <br> - Developed GNN models <br> - Created slides and presentation <br> - Built the Dash app <br> - Wrote and proofread the report <br> - Handled report formatting |
| Kai Truong | 530148559 | - Researched and compiled the Results section <br> - Delivered the final presentation <br> - Contributed to slides and report <br> - Refined and proofread the report <br> - Refined and worked on the research question |
| Robin Manjosh John | 540910768 | - Fixed and improved the dashboard <br> - Developed background for the research question <br> - Presented the final product <br> - Compiled the Background section <br> - Assisted in completing the Results section |
| Hancheng Qiu | 520622546 | - Developed the HAR baseline and HAR-X model with cross-model validation <br> - Answered Q&A during the presentation <br> - Wrote the HAR-X report section <br> - Collaborated on project scope improvements including GitHub commits and feedback |
| Jamie Khov | 540816954 | - Built and researched the GARCH model <br> - Formatted the report and integrated code into the `.qmd` file <br> - Delivered the final presentation <br> - Refined the References section |

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
## Appendix

#### Code Availability

The full source code, data processing pipeline, feature engineering,
model training scripts, and Dash application are available on GitHub:

<https://github.com/r4hibRT/DATA3888-Finance-21>

#### Appendix A: Feature Definitions

::: {#tbl-features}

| Feature | Definition | Formula |
|:--------|:-----------|:-------:|
| `wap` | Weighted average price using best bid/ask and opposite-side size weights | $\frac{\text{bid}_1 \cdot \text{ask\_sz}_1 + \text{ask}_1 \cdot \text{bid\_sz}_1}{\text{bid\_sz}_1 + \text{ask\_sz}_1}$ |
| `bid_ask_spread` | Relative spread between lowest ask and highest bid | $\text{ask}_1 - \text{bid}_1$ |
| `total_volume` | Total displayed volume across top two bid and ask levels | $\text{bid\_sz}_1 + \text{bid\_sz}_2 + \text{ask\_sz}_1 + \text{ask\_sz}_2$ |
| `price_spread` | Full price range across the top two bid and ask quotes | $\max(\text{price}) - \min(\text{price})$ |
| `depth_imbalance` | Relative imbalance between bid-side and ask-side depth | $\frac{(\text{bid\_sz}_1 + \text{bid\_sz}_2) - (\text{ask\_sz}_1 + \text{ask\_sz}_2)}{(\text{bid\_sz}_1 + \text{bid\_sz}_2) + (\text{ask\_sz}_1 + \text{ask\_sz}_2)}$ |
| `log_ret` | Log return of weighted average price | $\log\!\left(\tfrac{\text{wap}_t}{\text{wap}_{t-1}}\right)$ |

Feature engineering variables used in the analysis.
:::

#### Appendix B: EDA

```{python}
#| label: fig-stock_interaction
#| fig-cap: "Stock Interaction Summary"
#| echo: True

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.image as mpimg

img = mpimg.imread("../EDA_pictures/stock interaction.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.imshow(img)
ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

```

```{python}
#| label: fig-eda-target-distribution
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of log-realised volatility across all forecast windows."
#| echo: True

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.image as mpimg

img = mpimg.imread("../eda_pictures/eda_target_distribution.png")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.imshow(img)
ax.axis("off")

plt.show()
```

#### Appendix C: DashApp

```{python}
#| label: fig-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "Regime Heatmap tab of the DashApp"
#| echo: True

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.image as mpimg

img = mpimg.imread("../DashApp/regime_heatmap.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.imshow(img)
ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ranking
#| fig-cap: "Model Ranking tab of the DashApp"
#| echo: True

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.image as mpimg

img = mpimg.imread("../DashApp/model_ranking.png")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.imshow(img)
ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

```

#### Appendix D: Terminology


::: {#tbl-terms}
| Terminology | Description |
|:------------|:------------|
| Pairwise correlations | Measures the strength and direction of linear association between two variables. Identifies which stocks or features tend to move together. |
| Granger causality | Tests whether past values of one variable improve prediction of another beyond its own history. Detects predictive precedence, not true causation. |
| Lead-lag correlation | Examines whether movements in one series tend to precede movements in another. Useful for detecting delayed reactions or information spillover across stocks. |
| Pairwise Diebold-Mariano | Compares forecast accuracy between two models. Applied across model pairs to test whether performance differences are statistically significant. |
| Newey-West HAC | Corrects standard errors when data has autocorrelation or heteroskedasticity. Keeps statistical tests valid for time series data. |
| Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction | Adjusts for false positives when running many tests simultaneously. Less conservative than Bonferroni, making it practical for large-scale testing. |
| GBDT | Gradient Boosted Decision Trees — an ensemble method building trees sequentially, each correcting errors of the last. Strong performer on tabular data. |
| Mutual information filtering | Selects features by shared information with the target, capturing non-linear relationships. Removes low-value features to reduce noise. |
| Mamba | A sequence model that processes long sequences in linear time. An alternative to Transformers for capturing temporal patterns. |
| GATv2 | A graph neural network using learnable attention weights to focus on the most relevant neighbouring nodes. Models relational structure between entities. |
| Sigmoid-gated scalar | A learnable value squashed to $[0, 1]$ via sigmoid. Acts as a soft switch controlling a component's contribution in the model. |

Key terminology used throughout the project.
:::
